Question.content: Which one of options A-D best fills the gap in the sentence "The young doctor______ in our hospital."?

Explanation.content:
👨‍⚕️ Subject-Verb Agreement: Singular Subject

🔤 The sentence:
"The young doctor ___ in our hospital."
🧐 Why "works" is correct?
• Subject = The young doctor (third person SINGULAR)
• In present tense, singular subjects take verb + -s / -es
• works = correct singular form ✅
❌ Why the others are wrong:
• work = plural form (they work) ❌
• working = needs auxiliary verb (is working) ❌
• have worked = plural present perfect (they have worked) ❌
✓ Final Answer: "works" is correct.

Question.content: Which one of options A-D best fills the gap in the sentence "A number of the visitors______ entertained by the singers."?

Explanation.content:
📊 "A number of" = Plural Verb

🔤 The sentence:
"A number of the visitors ___ entertained by the singers."
🧐 Why "were" is correct?
• A number of + plural noun = takes a PLURAL verb ✅
• The real subject is "visitors" (plural), not "a number"
• Plural subject → plural verb: were ✅
• This is past tense context (entertained = past participle)
⚠️ Compare with "the number of":
• ✅ A number of students are late. (plural)
• ✅ The number of students is large. (singular)
This is a common test trick!
✓ Final Answer: "were" is correct.

Question.content: Which one of options A-D best fills the gap in the sentence "Those animals______ here."?

Explanation.content:
🦁 Plural Subject = Plural Verb

🔤 The sentence:
"Those animals ___ here."
🧐 Why "feed" is correct?
• Subject = Those animals (PLURAL)
• Plural subject → plural verb (no 's'): feed ✅
• The sentence is in present tense (habit/regular action)
❌ Why the others are wrong:
• feeds = singular verb (for "that animal") ❌
• fed = past tense (possible but "here" suggests present) ❌
• has been feeding = singular present perfect continuous ❌
✓ Final Answer: "feed" is correct.

Question.content: Which one of options A-D best fills the gap in the sentence "Many a citizen______ frustrated."?

Explanation.content:
📚 "Many a" = Singular!

🔤 The sentence:
"Many a citizen ___ frustrated."
🧐 Why "is" is correct?
• Many a + singular noun = SINGULAR meaning, even though it refers to many people!
• It emphasizes each individual rather than the group
• Singular subject → singular verb: is ✅
• "Many a citizen have" would be incorrect ❌
📋 More examples:
• ✅ Many a soldier has died in war.
• ✅ Many a student is struggling.
• ❌ Many a soldier have died.
• ❌ Many a student are struggling.
✓ Final Answer: "is" is correct.

Question.content: Which one of options A-D best fills the gap in the sentence "These girls ______ ice creams."?

Explanation.content:
👧 Plural Subject = Plural Verb

🔤 The sentence:
"These girls ___ ice creams."
🧐 Which is correct?
• Subject = These girls (plural)
• Plural subject → plural verb: love (no 's') ✅
• "loves" = singular ❌
• "loving" = needs auxiliary (are loving) ❌
• "has been loving" = singular and awkward ❌
✓ Final Answer: "love" is correct.

Question.content: Which one of options A-D best fills the gap in the sentence "The man and his wife ______ twins."?

Explanation.content:
👫 Compound Subject = Plural Verb

🔤 The sentence:
"The man and his wife ___ twins."
🧐 Which is correct?
• Subject = The man and his wife (2 people = PLURAL)
• Plural subject → plural verb: like (no 's') ✅
• "likes" = singular ❌
✓ Final Answer: "like" is correct.

Question.content: Which one of options A-D best fills the gap in the sentence "You ______ here every day."?

Explanation.content:
👤 You = Plural Verb

🔤 The sentence:
"You ___ here every day."
🧐 Which is correct?
• Subject = You (takes plural verb form)
• You always takes plural verb: come ✅
• "comes" = third person singular (he/she) ❌
• "comed" is not a word (past = came) ❌
✓ Final Answer: "come" is correct.

Question.content: Which one of options A-D best fills the gap in the sentence "I am wondering ______ to ask."?

Explanation.content:
❓ Who vs Whom

🔤 The sentence:
"I am wondering ___ to ask."
🧐 Which is correct?
• Whom = object pronoun (receives action) ✅
• Here, the person is the OBJECT of the verb "ask"
• Who = subject pronoun (performs action) ❌
• Trick: Answer with "him" = whom; "he" = who
• "I am wondering to ask him" → WHOM ✅
📋 Memory trick:
• Who = he/she (subject)
• Whom = him/her (object)
✓ Final Answer: "whom" is correct.

Question.content: Which one of options A-D best fills the gap in the sentence "This is a ______ appointment."?

Explanation.content:
📅 Temporary vs Temporal

🔤 The sentence:
"This is a ___ appointment."
🧐 Which is correct?
• temporary = lasting for a limited time ✅
• temporal = relating to time (not about duration) ❌
• "tempera" = paint type ❌
✓ Final Answer: "temporary" is correct.

Question.content: Which one of options A-D best fills the gap in the sentence "______ you should go with us."?

Explanation.content:
🔗 Conjunctive Adverbs

🔤 The sentence:
"___ you should go with us."
🧐 Which is correct?
• Nonetheless = "nevertheless; in spite of that" ✅
• Irregardless is non-standard (use "regardless") ❌
• Regarding = about/concerning (needs a subject) ❌
• Regard = verb/noun (not a connector) ❌
✓ Final Answer: "Nonetheless" is correct.

Question.content: Which of the sentences below has a rising tune?

Explanation.content:
🎵 Rising vs Falling Intonation

📈 Rising intonation:
• Voice pitch goes UP at the end ⬆️
• Used for YES/NO questions ✅
• "Is she coming?" ⬆️
📉 Falling intonation:
• Voice pitch goes DOWN at the end ⬇️
• Used for statements, commands, WH-questions
• "Boma bought a new car." ⬇️
• "He scored a hat-trick." ⬇️
• "Don't touch it!" ⬇️
✓ Final Answer: "Is she coming?" has a rising tune (yes/no question).

Question.content: The act of communication in which information is transferred from a transmitter to a receiver is called

Explanation.content:
✍️ Writing as Communication

📝 Breaking it down:
• Transmitter = the writer (sender)
• Receiver = the reader (audience)
• Information transferred = the message
• This describes the act of WRITING ✅
❌ Why not others?
• Reading = receiver decoding, not transmitting
• Speaker = oral communication, not writing
• Decoding = interpreting, not transmitting
✓ Final Answer: "writing" is correct.

Question.content: The central idea in a paragraph is usually captured in a

Explanation.content:
📝 Topic Sentence = Paragraph's Main Idea

🧐 What's the difference?
• Topic sentence = main idea of a PARAGRAPH ✅
• Thesis statement = main idea of an entire ESSAY 📘
• Concluding paragraph = wraps up the essay 🔚
• Context clue = hints to understand words 🔍
✓ Final Answer: Topic sentence captures the central idea of a paragraph.

Question.content: Pick out the odd item from the following list of words:

Explanation.content:
📋 Elements vs. Unit

🔍 Analyzing each word:
• Organization = quality of good writing ✅
• Content = quality of good writing ✅
• Mechanical accuracy = quality of good writing (grammar, spelling) ✅
• Paragraph = a UNIT of writing, not a quality ❌
🎯 The odd one out:
Organization, Content, and Mechanical accuracy are all qualities/elements of effective writing.
Paragraph is the structure/unit itself.
✓ Final Answer: "Paragraph" is the odd one out.

Question.content: Pick out the odd item from the following list of words:

Explanation.content:
📐 Paragraph Formats

🔍 Analyzing each word:
• Hanging paragraph = first line flush left, subsequent lines indented ✅
• Intended paragraph = first line indented (probably meant "Indented") ✅
• Block paragraph = all lines flush left, no indentation ✅
• Headed paragraph = NOT a standard paragraph format! ❌
🎯 The odd one out:
"Headed paragraph" is not a recognized paragraph format. The correct term is likely "Indented paragraph."
✓ Final Answer: "Headed paragraph" is the odd one out.

Question.content: Pick out the odd item from the following list of words:

Explanation.content:
✅ Qualities vs ❌ Errors

🔍 Analyzing each word:
• Unity = GOOD quality (all sentences support one idea) ✅
• Sentence variation = GOOD quality (mix short/long sentences) ✅
• Focus = GOOD quality (clear main idea) ✅
• Sentence fragments = ERROR (incomplete sentences) ❌
🎯 The odd one out:
Unity, sentence variation, and focus are all positive qualities of good writing.
Sentence fragments are writing errors to avoid.
✓ Final Answer: "Sentence fragments" is the odd one out (error, not quality).

Question.content: Pick out the odd item from the following list of words:

Explanation.content:
🔗 Additive vs Contrastive Connectors

🔍 Analyzing each word:
• In addition = ADDITIVE (adds information) ✅
• Also = ADDITIVE (adds information) ✅
• Moreover = ADDITIVE (adds information) ✅
• However = CONTRASTIVE (shows opposition) ❌
🎯 The odd one out:
"In addition," "also," and "moreover" all mean "and" — they add similar ideas.
"However" means "but" — it shows contrast/opposition.
✓ Final Answer: "However" is the odd one out (contrast vs addition).

Question.content: Where may topic sentences occur in paragraphs?

Explanation.content:
📍 Flexible Topic Sentence Placement

📋 Possible positions:
• Beginning = most common (deductive) ✅
• Middle = less common but possible ✅
• End = inductive paragraph (build up to main idea) ✅
• Implied = not directly stated, but understood ✅
🎯 Correct answer:
Topic sentences can appear ANYWHERE in a paragraph — beginning, middle, or end!
✓ Final Answer: Topic sentences may occur ANYWHERE in a paragraph.

Question.content: Which of the following may be used in paragraph development?

Explanation.content:
📚 Methods of Paragraph Development

✅ ALL are valid:
• Personal experience = anecdotes, stories from one's life ✅
• Experience from history = historical examples, facts ✅
• Illustrations = examples, pictures, diagrams ✅
🎯 Correct answer:
All of these are valid methods to develop and support ideas in a paragraph!
✓ Final Answer: All of the above can be used in paragraph development.

Question.content: Which paragraph format is reserved for citing longer texts from sources?

Explanation.content:
📖 Block Quotations

🧐 What is a block paragraph?
• Also called a block quotation
• Used for longer citations (usually 40+ words or 4+ lines)
• Indented from the left margin (sometimes both sides)
• No quotation marks needed — the formatting shows it's a quote
❌ Not the others:
• Hanging paragraph = first line out, others in (bibliographies)
• Intended (Indented) paragraph = first line in, others out
• Short paragraph = length, not format
✓ Final Answer: Block paragraph is for citing longer texts.

Question.content: Which variety of language does speechwriting require?

Explanation.content:
🎤 Speechwriting = Formal

🧐 Why formal?
• Speeches are usually delivered to an audience in formal settings
• Requires careful word choice, complete sentences, proper grammar
• Avoids slang, contractions, and casual expressions
• Examples: political speeches, graduation addresses, keynote speeches
✓ Final Answer: Speechwriting requires FORMAL language.

Question.content: Which variety of language is required in a letter to an acquaintance?

Explanation.content:
✉️ Letter to an Acquaintance = Semi-formal

🧐 What is semi-formal?
• Between formal and informal
• Someone you know but not very close (neighbor, colleague, family friend)
• Polite but not stiff; friendly but not too casual
• Use: Dear Mr./Ms., complete sentences, avoid slang
📋 Levels of formality:
• Formal = official, business, strangers
• Semi-formal = acquaintances ✅
• Informal/Colloquial = close friends, family
✓ Final Answer: Letter to an acquaintance requires SEMI-FORMAL language.

Question.content: Which of the following will be odd in an introductory paragraph?
options: Thesis statement, Background of topic and Courting audience interest

Explanation.content:
📝 What Belongs in an Introduction?

✅ Introduction should have:
• Hook / Courting audience interest
• Background of topic
• Thesis statement
❌ What is ODD:
Developing ideas only — This belongs in the BODY paragraphs, not the introduction!
Introductions introduce; body paragraphs develop.
✓ Final Answer: "Developing ideas only" is odd in an introductory paragraph.

Question.content: Which of the following represents qualities of effective writing?

Explanation.content:
✨ Qualities of Effective Writing

✅ GOOD qualities:
• Economy = using the fewest words possible to convey meaning (no wordiness) 💰
• Simplicity = clear, easy to understand language (not complicated) 🌟
• Conciseness = brief but complete; getting to the point quickly ✂️
❌ What to AVOID:
• Complexity = unnecessarily complicated, hard to follow ❌
• Verbosity = wordiness, using too many words (opposite of economy) ❌
• Conciseness alone = not enough; you need simplicity and economy too
💡 Memory tip:
Good writing = Say what you need to say, clearly and briefly, without wasting words.
✓ Final Answer: Economy, Simplicity, and Conciseness are qualities of effective writing.

Question.content: What variety of language is used in the sentence "Two guys came to see my dad last night."?

Explanation.content:
🗣️ Informal Language Features

🔤 The sentence:
"Two guys came to see my dad last night."
🧐 Why is this informal?
• Guys = casual term for men (formal would be "gentlemen" or "persons")
• My dad = familiar, family term (formal would be "my father")
• Conversational, everyday speech pattern
• No complex vocabulary or structure
📋 Compare formality levels:
• Formal: "Two gentlemen visited my father yesterday evening."
• Informal: "Two guys came to see my dad last night." ✅
✓ Final Answer: INFORMAL language.

Question.content: What variety of language is used in the sentence "They had a really long talk about the whole thing."?

Explanation.content:
🗣️ Informal Language Features

🔤 The sentence:
"They had a really long talk about the whole thing."
🧐 Why is this informal?
• Really = informal intensifier (formal would be "very" or "extremely") • The whole thing = vague, casual reference (formal would be "the entire matter" or "the whole issue") • Conversational, everyday speech pattern
📋 Compare:
• Formal: "They had a very lengthy discussion about the entire matter."
• Informal: "They had a really long talk about the whole thing." ✅
✓ Final Answer: INFORMAL language.

Question.content: What variety of language is used in the sentence "The government has sorted out a new peace deal with the whole militants."?

Explanation.content:
🗣️ Informal Language + Grammar Error

🔤 The sentence:
"The government has sorted out a new peace deal with the whole militants."
🧐 Why is this informal?
• Sorted out = phrasal verb, very casual (formal: "resolved," "negotiated," "concluded") • The whole militants = grammatical error! Should be "all the militants" • Informal/casual register with non-standard grammar
📋 Compare with formal version:
• Formal: "The government has brokered a new peace deal with the militants." (see Q328)
• Informal: "The government has sorted out a new peace deal with the whole militants." ✅ (with error)
✓ Final Answer: INFORMAL language.

Question.content: What variety of language is used in the sentence "The government has brokered a new peace deal with the militants."?

Explanation.content:
📝 Formal Language Features

🔤 The sentence:
"The government has brokered a new peace deal with the militants."
🧐 Why is this formal?
• Brokered = sophisticated, formal vocabulary (not "sorted out") • Complete, grammatically correct sentence • Appropriate for news reporting, official statements, academic writing • No slang, no contractions, no grammatical errors
📋 Compare with informal version:
• Formal: "The government has brokered a new peace deal..." ✅
• Informal: "The government has sorted out a new peace deal..." (Q327)
Same topic, different register!
✓ Final Answer: FORMAL language.

Question.content: What variety of language is used in the sentence "We considered lots of options but we've got to take only one, and we did."?

Explanation.content:
🗣️ Informal Language Features

🔤 The sentence:
"We considered lots of options but we've got to take only one, and we did."
🧐 Why informal?
• Lots of = informal (formal: many/several)
• We've got to = contraction + informal phrase (formal: we must/we have to)
• Conversational, everyday speech pattern
✓ Final Answer: INFORMAL language.

Question.content: What variety of language is used in the sentence "A decision was finally reached, but not before we had considered several options."?

Explanation.content:
📝 Formal Language Features

🔤 The sentence:
"A decision was finally reached, but not before we had considered several options."
🧐 Why formal?
• Was finally reached = passive voice (common in formal writing)
• Several = formal (compare to "lots of" in Q329)
• No contractions, complete sentences, sophisticated vocabulary
• Official, academic, or professional register
✓ Final Answer: FORMAL language.

Question.content: What variety of language is used in the sentence "I spent my last Easter holidays with my best friend, George, and his parents."?

Explanation.content:
📝 Semi-Formal Language

🔤 The sentence:
"I spent my last Easter holidays with my best friend, George, and his parents."
🧐 Why semi-formal?
• Complete, grammatically correct sentence
• Personal but not overly casual
• Uses "best friend" (informal) but proper punctuation and structure
• Appropriate for personal letters, diaries, narratives
✓ Final Answer: SEMI-FORMAL language.

Question.content: To which of the following sensory organs should a good descriptive text appeal to?

Explanation.content:
👃👁️👂 Descriptive Writing = Sensory Details

🎯 All five senses!
• Sight (eye) - what things look like
• Hearing (ear) - sounds
• Smell (nose) - odors, scents
• Touch (skin) - texture, temperature
• Taste (tongue) - flavors
✅ The best answer:
"Eye, nose and skin" (sight, smell, touch) - but a truly good descriptive text appeals to ALL senses! The test expects this combination.
✓ Final Answer: Good descriptive text should appeal to eye, nose and skin (and more!).

Question.content: Which of the following activities is NOT involved in pre-writing?

Explanation.content:
✍️ Stages of Writing Process

📋 Pre-writing (Brainstorming stage):
• Interviews - gathering information ✅
• Library research - finding sources ✅
• Field work - observations, data collection ✅
❌ What comes LATER:
Editing = happens AFTER drafting (revising, proofreading) ❌
📌 Writing Process:
Pre-writing → Drafting → Revising → Editing → Publishing
✓ Final Answer: EDITING is NOT involved in pre-writing.

Question.content: Paragraph is best described as a chunk of thought in a/an ______

Explanation.content:
📝 Paragraph = Chunk of Thought in Composition

🧐 Breaking it down:
• Paragraph = a unit of writing
• Chunk of thought = one main idea developed
• Composition = a written work (essay, article, etc.)
• Paragraphs are the building blocks of a COMPOSITION ✅
✓ Final Answer: Paragraph is a chunk of thought in a COMPOSITION.

Question.content: ______ is usually a group of related sentences that express one major idea.

Explanation.content:
📋 Definition of a Paragraph

🔍 Let's check each option:
• Essay = multiple paragraphs on one topic ❌
• Article = longer work, may have multiple sections ❌
• Topic sentence = ONE sentence, not a group ❌
• Paragraph = group of related sentences expressing ONE major idea ✅
💡 Memory trick:
• Paragraph = group of sentences about ONE idea
• Topic sentence = the sentence that states that idea
✓ Final Answer: PARAGRAPH is a group of related sentences expressing one major idea.

Question.content: One of the following is not a quality of a good paragraph.

Explanation.content:
✅ Qualities of a Good Paragraph

📋 The FOUR qualities:
• Unity = one main idea ✅
• Coherence = logical flow, easy to follow ✅
• Completeness = fully developed with supporting details ✅
• Eligibility = ❌ NOT a paragraph quality! (Eligibility means being qualified for something)
🎯 The odd one out:
Unity, Coherence, and Completeness are the three main qualities of a good paragraph (sometimes also Emphasis/Order). Eligibility is completely unrelated!
✓ Final Answer: "Eligibility" is NOT a quality of a good paragraph.

Question.content: Words combine to form ______ which in turn combine to form a paragraph.

Explanation.content:
📚 Hierarchy of Language

🔤 The correct sequence:
Letters → Words → Sentences → Paragraphs → Essays/Compositions
🧐 Why not phrases?
• Words combine to form phrases AND sentences
• But it's sentences that directly combine to form paragraphs ✅
• Paragraphs are groups of SENTENCES, not phrases
✓ Final Answer: Words combine to form SENTENCES, which combine to form paragraphs.

Question.content: ______ is when the sentences in a paragraph are all clearly related and closely connected to the main idea.

Explanation.content:
🎯 Unity = One Main Idea

📋 Understanding the terms:
• Unity = all sentences support ONE main idea; no irrelevant sentences ✅
• Cohesion = grammatical connections between sentences (transition words, pronouns)
• Legibility = handwriting is clear/readable
• Adequate development = paragraph is fully explained
💡 Memory trick:
UNITY = ONE idea = all sentences UNITED around the topic sentence.
✓ Final Answer: UNITY is when sentences are related and connected to the main idea.

Question.content: The smooth progression of ideas from one sentence to another is referred to as ______

Explanation.content:
🔄 Coherence vs Cohesion

🧐 What's the difference?
• Coherence = ideas flow logically, make sense together (smooth progression) ✅
• Cohesion = grammar links (pronouns, transition words, repetition)
📝 Simple analogy:
• Coherence = the paragraph "makes sense" as a whole
• Cohesion = the glue that holds sentences together
• The question asks about "smooth progression of ideas" = COHERENCE ✅
✓ Final Answer: Smooth progression of ideas = COHERENCE.

Question.content: The following are types of paragraph except ______

Explanation.content:
📑 Types of Paragraphs by Function

✅ Functional paragraph types:
• Introductory paragraph = opens an essay, presents thesis ✅
• Transitional paragraph = moves from one idea to another (short) ✅
• Concluding paragraph = closes an essay, restates thesis ✅
• Body paragraphs = develop main ideas (not listed here)
❌ The fake one:
Compositional — NOT a type of paragraph! A composition is the WHOLE essay, not a paragraph type.
✓ Final Answer: "Compositional" is NOT a type of paragraph.

Question.content: It is through paragraphing that ______ and ______ are provided in any composition.

Explanation.content:
📋 Purpose of Paragraphing

🧐 Why do we use paragraphs?
• Organization = structure the content logically ✅
• Focus = each paragraph has ONE main idea ✅
• Also provides: readability, visual breaks, clarity
❌ Why not others?
• Academic/writing = too vague
• Topic/sentence = these are parts OF a paragraph
• Thesis/statement = thesis is for the whole essay
✓ Final Answer: Paragraphing provides ORGANIZATION and FOCUS.

Question.content: According to Ojo and Umera-Okeke, the following are parts of a paragraph except

Explanation.content:
📚 Parts of a Paragraph

✅ The THREE main parts:
1. Topic sentence = states the main idea ✅
2. Supporting details = develop/explain the idea ✅
3. Concluding sentence = wraps up the paragraph ✅
❌ The exception:
Manageable chunks — This is a description of what paragraphs ARE (chunks of thought), not a structural PART of a paragraph.
✓ Final Answer: "Manageable chunks" is NOT a part of a paragraph.

Question.content: The topic sentence is the sentence which contains the ______ idea of a paragraph.

Explanation.content:
🎯 Controlling Idea

🧐 What is a "controlling idea"?
• The controlling idea is the specific point/attitude about the topic
• It "controls" or limits what the paragraph will discuss
• Example: "Dogs make excellent pets because they are loyal."
   - Topic = dogs
   - Controlling idea = make excellent pets because they are loyal
📋 Terminology:
• Topic sentence = contains BOTH the topic AND the controlling idea ✅
• Not "comprehension," "indented," or "developing"
✓ Final Answer: The topic sentence contains the CONTROLLING idea.

Question.content: Which of these is TRUE of the length of a paragraph?
options: The length is as important as the sense
The best length should be five lines or sentences
No rule legislates the number of sentences
Verbosity determines the length of a paragraph

Answer.content: No rule legislates the number of sentences

Explanation.content:
📏 Paragraph Length: No Fixed Rule

🧐 What determines paragraph length?
• Content and purpose — not a fixed number of sentences!
• A paragraph can be ONE sentence or MANY sentences
• What matters is UNITY (one main idea), not length
✅ The correct answer:
"No rule legislates the number of sentences" — This is TRUE! ✅
There is no grammar rule that says a paragraph must have 3, 5, or any specific number of sentences.
✓ Final Answer: No rule legislates the number of sentences in a paragraph.

Question.content: The topic sentence is also called the ______.

Explanation.content:
🌰 Topic Sentence = Kernel Sentence

🧐 What is a kernel sentence?
• A kernel sentence is the core/basic sentence that contains the main idea
• In paragraph writing, the topic sentence is sometimes called the kernel sentence
• It's the "seed" from which the rest of the paragraph grows 🌱
❌ Why not others?
• Thesis statement = main idea of an ESSAY, not paragraph
• Terminal position = location (end of paragraph)
• Supporting sentence = develops the topic sentence
✓ Final Answer: The topic sentence is also called the KERNEL SENTENCE.

Question.content: There is nothing wrong with locating your topic sentence in the middle of a paragraph.

Explanation.content:
📍 Topic Sentence Placement

📋 Possible positions:
• Beginning = most common (deductive) ✅
• Middle = less common but perfectly acceptable ✅
• End = inductive paragraph (build up to main idea) ✅
• Implied = not directly stated ✅
✅ The statement is TRUE:
There is absolutely nothing wrong with placing the topic sentence in the middle. It's a matter of style and purpose, not correctness!
✓ Final Answer: TRUE — topic sentences can be placed anywhere.

Question.content: Regardless of your department, ______ is the chief means by which you will be examined by your lecturer.

Explanation.content:
✍️ How Are Students Examined?

🧐 Think about it:
• Exams = you WRITE answers 📝
• Essays, term papers, assignments = WRITING 📄
• Even oral exams are rare; written exams are the norm
• Reading/listening/speaking are skills, but examination is done through WRITING ✅
✓ Final Answer: WRITING is the chief means of examination.

Question.content: Academic papers come in such forms as long essays, seminars, and ______.

Explanation.content:
📚 Types of Academic Papers

📋 Common academic paper forms:
• Long essays = extended written assignments ✅
• Seminars = oral presentations with written components ✅
• Term papers = research papers at end of term ✅
• Also: theses, dissertations, research articles, reports
❌ Not the others:
• Letter writing = personal/business correspondence ❌
• Departmental courses = classes, not papers ❌
• Composition variety = vague, not a specific type ❌
✓ Final Answer: Academic papers include long essays, seminars, and TERM PAPERS.

Question.content: A ______ is an academic essay of medium length for which a topic has been assigned or chosen?

Explanation.content:
📝 Definition of a Term Paper

🧐 Key features:
• Academic essay = formal writing
• Medium length = not too short, not a full thesis
• Topic assigned or chosen = you get a topic or pick one
• Usually submitted at the end of a term/semester
✅ This describes a TERM PAPER:
Exactly! This is the standard definition of a term paper.
✓ Final Answer: TERM PAPER is the correct term.

Question.content: In any piece of composition, the pre-writing stage entails ______.
options: Brainstorming on a topic and outlining points
Getting a topic and writing the first draft
Drawing up a table of content and proofreading
Editing the draft and polishing it

Answer.content: Brainstorming on a topic and outlining points

Explanation.content:
💡 Pre-writing = Getting Ready to Write

📋 Pre-writing activities include:
• Brainstorming = generating ideas 💭
• Outlining = organizing ideas into a structure 📋
• Research, gathering information
• Freewriting, clustering, listing
❌ What comes LATER:
• Writing the first draft = WRITING stage ❌
• Proofreading, editing = POST-WRITING stage ❌
• Table of contents = after drafting ❌
✓ Final Answer: Pre-writing = BRAINSTORMING and OUTLINING.

Question.content: The ______ stage of Term Paper Writing is where you put pen to paper.

Explanation.content:
✍️ The Writing Stage

📝 The 3 Main Stages:
1. Pre-writing = planning, brainstorming 📋
2. Writing = putting pen to paper, drafting ✍️✅
3. Post-writing = revising, editing, proofreading 🔍
🧐 "Put pen to paper" means:
Actually writing the first draft! This is the WRITING stage.
✓ Final Answer: WRITING stage is where you put pen to paper.

Question.content: The Post-writing stage of Term Paper Writing is otherwise called the ______ stage.

Explanation.content:
📝 The Three Stages of Writing

📋 Writing Process Stages:
• Pre-writing = planning, brainstorming 📋
• Writing = drafting, putting pen to paper ✍️
• Post-writing / Re-writing = revising, editing, proofreading 🔍✅
🧐 Why "Re-writing"?
Post-writing is also called the re-writing stage because you go back to improve, revise, and polish your work. It's not just writing once — you rewrite to make it better!
✓ Final Answer: Post-writing = RE-WRITING stage.

Question.content: Academic writing can take any of the following forms except ______

Explanation.content:
📚 Forms of Academic Writing

✅ Academic writing includes:
• Seminars = research presentations with written papers ✅
• Term papers = end-of-term research essays ✅
• Dissertations = lengthy research for degrees ✅
• Essays, theses, research articles, journals, etc.
❌ The EXCEPTION:
Report cards — These are NOT academic writing! They are administrative documents that report grades, not scholarly work.
✓ Final Answer: Report cards are NOT a form of academic writing.

Question.content: ______ simply means to steal someone else's ideas and pass them off as your own.

Explanation.content:
🚫 Plagiarism = Academic Theft

📋 Definitions:
• Plagiarism = using someone else's work/ideas without credit ✅
• Malapropism = using wrong word ("dance a flamingo" instead of "flamenco") ❌
• Tautology = saying same thing twice ("free gift") ❌
• Pleonasm = using more words than needed ("burning fire") ❌
⚠️ Serious academic offense!
Plagiarism can get you failed, expelled, or even sued. Always cite your sources!
✓ Final Answer: PLAGIARISM is stealing others' ideas.

Question.content: In any academic writing, it is advisable for the writer to use evidence that is reliable, accurate and ______

Explanation.content:
🎯 Qualities of Good Evidence

✅ Evidence should be:
• Reliable = consistent, trustworthy ✅
• Accurate = correct, factual ✅
• Objective = unbiased, not influenced by personal feelings ✅
❌ What to AVOID:
• Biased = one-sided, unfair ❌
• Unverifiable = cannot be checked/confirmed ❌
• Comprehension = understanding (not a quality of evidence) ❌
✓ Final Answer: Evidence should be reliable, accurate, and OBJECTIVE.

Question.content: Sources of information for academic writing are broadly divided into ______ and ______ sources.

Explanation.content:
📚 Types of Information Sources

📋 Two broad categories:
• Print sources = physical/paper format 📖
   Books, journals, newspapers, magazines, encyclopedias
• Electronic sources = digital format 💻
   Websites, e-books, online journals, databases, PDFs
✅ This is the most common broad division:
Print vs Electronic (also called Online vs Offline).
✓ Final Answer: Sources are divided into PRINT and ELECTRONIC.

Question.content: Academic journals are usually ______

Explanation.content:
🔬 Peer-Reviewed Journals

🧐 What does "peer-reviewed" mean?
• Before publication, the article is reviewed by experts in the same field (peers)
• They check for accuracy, methodology, significance
• They recommend: accept, revise, or reject
• This ensures quality and credibility ✅
📝 Also called:
• Refereed journals
• Scholarly journals
• Academic journals
✓ Final Answer: Academic journals are usually PEER-REVIEWED.

Question.content: In academic writing, information is grouped into ______

Explanation.content:
📊 Types of Information Sources

📋 The three categories:
• Primary sources = original, firsthand evidence 🔬
   Research studies, interviews, experiments, diaries, speeches
• Secondary sources = analyze/interpret primary sources 📘
   Textbooks, reviews, critiques, biographies
• Tertiary sources = compile/index primary/secondary 📚
   Encyclopedias, databases, bibliographies
✓ Final Answer: Information is grouped into PRIMARY, SECONDARY, and TERTIARY.

Question.content: Which of the sentences below is true?
options: Intonation is not necessary for a proper English speech.
There is only one acceptable definition for intonation.
Intonation is another word for stress.

Explanation.content:
🎵 Intonation and Pitch

🔍 Analyzing each option:
• ✅ Pitch modulation carries extra-linguistic meanings = TRUE!
   Pitch changes show emotion, attitude, sarcasm, etc. (not just dictionary meaning)
• ❌ Intonation is not necessary = FALSE! Every English sentence has intonation
• ❌ Only one definition = FALSE! Intonation has multiple definitions/aspects
• ❌ Intonation = stress = FALSE! Stress is emphasis on syllables; intonation is pitch pattern
✓ Final Answer: Pitch modulation carries extra-linguistic meanings is TRUE.

Question.content: The statement, "No language is spoken in monotones" means:
options: All languages use pitch modulations to convey extra-linguistic nuances.
No language uses monotone pitch modulations.
No two languages are the same.

Answer.content: All languages use pitch modulations to convey extra-linguistic nuances.

Explanation.content:
🎵 No Language is Spoken in Monotones

🧐 What does this mean?
• Monotone = speaking with NO pitch variation, all on one note
• The statement says: No language is spoken like that
• Therefore: ALL languages have pitch variation/intonation
• This pitch variation conveys extra-linguistic nuances (emotion, attitude, etc.)
✅ The correct interpretation:
"All languages use pitch modulations to convey extra-linguistic nuances."
This is a universal feature of human language!
✓ Final Answer: All languages use pitch modulations to convey extra-linguistic nuances.

Question.content: Commands or imperative sentences take a ______

Explanation.content:
📢 Intonation for Commands

🎵 Falling tune ⬇️
• Commands/Imperatives = voice goes DOWN at the end
• Shows authority, finality, certainty
• Examples: "Sit down." ⬇️ "Close the door." ⬇️ "Stop!" ⬇️
🔄 Compare:
• Falling tune = commands, statements, WH-questions ✅
• Rising tune = yes/no questions, politeness ⬆️
• Rise-fall = surprise, strong emotion
✓ Final Answer: Commands take a FALLING TUNE.

Question.content: The intonation tune for the tag question, "He does not like driving, does he?" will be a/an

Explanation.content:
❓ Tag Question Intonation

🎵 Two possible patterns:
• Falling ⬇️ + Rising ⬆️ = speaker is uncertain, genuinely asking ✅
   "He doesn't like driving, does he?" (I'm not sure, please tell me)
• Falling ⬇️ + Falling ⬇️ = speaker is certain, just confirming
   "He doesn't like driving, does he?" (I already know, just checking)
✅ The question asks for the tune:
The tag question typically has a falling/rising pattern — the statement falls, the tag rises.
✓ Final Answer: Tag question = FALLING/RISING tune.

Question.content: The language of the literature of knowledge is expository and

Explanation.content:
📚 Literature of Knowledge vs Power

🧐 Thomas De Quincey's distinction:
• Literature of Knowledge = teaches, informs, explains
   → Language is expository and DENOTATIVE (literal, dictionary meaning) ✅
• Literature of Power = moves, inspires, imagines
   → Language is connotative, metaphorical, figurative
📖 Examples:
• Knowledge: textbooks, scientific papers, encyclopedias
• Power: poetry, novels, drama
✓ Final Answer: Literature of knowledge = expository and DENOTATIVE.

Question.content: Literature of knowledge deals with ___ and figures, as in History, Economics, Biology and Chemistry

Explanation.content:
📊 Literature of Knowledge = Facts

🧐 What does it deal with?
• Facts = verifiable information, data, truths ✅
• Figures = statistics, numbers, data points
• History = dates, events, evidence
• Economics = GDP, inflation, employment rates
• Biology = cells, DNA, species classification
• Chemistry = elements, compounds, reactions
✅ The correct word:
"Literature of knowledge deals with facts and figures."
Not fair, freedom, or free — those are abstract concepts, not the core of knowledge literature.
✓ Final Answer: Literature of knowledge deals with FACTS and figures.

Question.content: The language of the literature of power is mainly

Explanation.content:
🎭 Literature of Power = Connotative

🧐 Understanding the difference:
• Connotative = emotional, associative, figurative meanings ✅
   Words suggest more than their dictionary definition
• Denotative = literal, dictionary meaning (Literature of Knowledge)
• Expository = explains, informs (Knowledge)
• Narrative = tells a story (can be either)
📖 Example:
• "Home" denotative = a place where one lives
• "Home" connotative = warmth, family, safety, belonging
Literature of power uses the SECOND meaning!
✓ Final Answer: Literature of power = CONNOTATIVE language.

Question.content: Literature of power is simply

Explanation.content:
💫 Literature of Power = Imaginative Literature

📚 Thomas De Quincey's classification:
• Literature of Power = imaginative literature ✅
   Moves the heart, stirs emotions, appeals to imagination
   Includes: poetry, novels, plays, creative writing
• Literature of Knowledge = informative literature
   Teaches facts, informs the mind
✅ Simply put:
Literature of power = IMAGINATIVE LITERATURE = creative writing = belles-lettres
✓ Final Answer: Literature of power = IMAGINATIVE LITERATURE.

Question.content: Another phrase for imaginative literature is

Explanation.content:
🔄 Imaginative Literature = Literature of Power

🧐 Same thing, different name:
• Imaginative literature = creative writing, fiction, poetry, drama
• Literature of power = Thomas De Quincey's term for the SAME thing ✅
• It has the power to move, inspire, transform the reader
❌ Not the others:
• Literature of Achebe/Elechi Amadi = specific authors, not a general category
• Literature of knowledge = opposite (informative, not imaginative)
✓ Final Answer: Imaginative literature = LITERATURE OF POWER.

Question.content: ______ literature is what gave birth to modern African literature

Explanation.content:
🗣️ Oral Literature: The Foundation of Modern African Literature

🧐 What is oral literature?
• Oral literature = spoken, performed, passed down through generations before writing
• Includes: folktales, myths, legends, proverbs, riddles, songs, epics
• This is the root of modern African literature ✅
📚 Famous African writers drew from oral traditions:
• Chinua Achebe — Igbo folktales and proverbs
• Wole Soyinka — Yoruba myths and rituals
• Ngũgĩ wa Thiong'o — Gikuyu oral narratives
✓ Final Answer: ORAL literature gave birth to modern African literature.

Question.content: Modern African literature evolved from the following, EXCEPT

Explanation.content:
🌍 Roots of Modern African Literature

✅ Sources of African literature:
• Story-telling = folktales, myths, legends ✅
• Rituals = ceremonies, festivals, performances ✅
• Incantations = chants, prayers, spells ✅
❌ The EXCEPTION:
Farming — This is an economic activity, not a literary or cultural form that directly evolved into literature. While farming may appear in literature, it is NOT a source/genre that literature evolved FROM.
✓ Final Answer: FARMING is the exception.

Question.content: The primary functions of literature is ______

Explanation.content:
📖 Functions of Literature

🎯 Primary functions:
• Entertainment = to delight, amuse, engage the reader/viewer ✅
• Education = to inform, teach, enlighten
• Social criticism = to correct, expose societal ills
• Cultural preservation = to document traditions
💡 Why "entertainment" is primary?
Before anything else, literature must first capture interest and engage the audience. Even serious messages are wrapped in entertaining stories!
✓ Final Answer: The primary function of literature is ENTERTAINMENT.

Question.content: Literature of power educates and ______

Explanation.content:
🎭 Literature of Power: Educate and Amuse

🧐 The dual purpose:
• Educates = teaches moral lessons, reveals truths about life ✅
• Amuses = entertains, delights, provides pleasure ✅
• This is the classical Horatian idea: "dulce et utile" (sweet and useful)
📖 Example:
A novel like Things Fall Apart educates about Igbo culture AND amuses through storytelling.
✓ Final Answer: Literature of power educates and AMUSES.

Question.content: Prose fiction includes such forms as the following EXCEPT

Explanation.content:
📚 Forms of Prose Fiction

✅ Prose fiction includes:
• The novel = long fictional narrative ✅
• The novella = medium-length fiction (between novel and short story) ✅
• The short story = brief fictional narrative ✅
❌ The EXCEPTION:
The dirge — This is a poem/song of mourning, not prose fiction! It belongs to poetry, not prose.
✓ Final Answer: The DIRGE is NOT a form of prose fiction.

Question.content: Autobiography is more of a ______ narrative than prose fiction

Explanation.content:
📜 Autobiography = Historical Narrative

🧐 Autobiography vs Fiction:
• Autobiography = true story of a person's life, written by that person
   Based on historical facts and real events ✅
• Prose fiction = invented/imaginative story
   Not bound by historical accuracy
✅ Autobiography is a HISTORICAL narrative:
It documents actual events, people, and periods from the author's life.
✓ Final Answer: Autobiography is more of a HISTORICAL narrative.

Question.content: Which is the odd one out?

Explanation.content:
📚 Novels by Chinua Achebe

🔍 Analyzing each work:
• Things Fall Apart = Chinua Achebe (1958) ✅
• Arrow of God = Chinua Achebe (1964) ✅
• The Falsified Road = Chinua Achebe (??? Actually this seems incorrect - Achebe didn't write this)
• The Ascent Stone = NOT by Chinua Achebe! ❌
🎯 The odd one out:
The Ascent Stone is not a novel by Chinua Achebe. The other three are his major works (though The Falsified Road seems misidentified - possibly a test error).
✓ Final Answer: The Ascent Stone is the odd one out.

Question.content: Which is not part of the outline for reading narrative fiction?

Explanation.content:
📖 Outline for Reading Narrative Fiction

✅ Typical outline elements:
• Context = historical, social, cultural background ✅
• Plot overview = summary of events/storyline ✅
• Character analysis = study of characters' traits, motivations ✅
• Theme, setting, point of view, style, etc.
❌ The EXCEPTION:
Suspense — This is an element/technique used IN the story, not a section of an outline for reading/analyzing narrative fiction.
✓ Final Answer: SUSPENSE is not part of the outline for reading narrative fiction.

Question.content: One unique feature of narrative fiction is that it communicates through the use of ______.

Explanation.content:
📖 Narrative Fiction = Storytelling

🧐 What is narrative fiction?
• Narrative fiction = imaginative storytelling (novels, short stories, etc.)
• Its unique feature = it communicates through STORIES ✅
• Songs, dancing, and protocol belong to other forms (poetry, drama, non-fiction)
✓ Final Answer: Narrative fiction communicates through STORIES.

Question.content: ______ is like the element that enables the writer of narrative fiction and drama to achieve order or pattern in his/her work.

Explanation.content:
📊 Plot = Order and Pattern

🧐 Plot vs Story:
• Story = sequence of events in chronological order
• Plot = the organized, structured arrangement of events ✅
• Plot gives order and pattern — beginning, middle, end; cause and effect; suspense; climax
📋 E.M. Forster's definition:
"The king died and then the queen died" = story.
"The king died, and then the queen died of grief" = plot.
✓ Final Answer: PLOT enables order and pattern.

Question.content: ______ is the soul of a work of fiction or play.

Explanation.content:
💫 Theme = The Soul of Literature

🧐 Why is theme the soul?
• Theme = the central idea, message, or insight about life ✅
• It is the soul because it gives the work meaning and purpose
• Plot = body; Characters = limbs; Theme = the spirit that brings it all to life
📖 Examples of themes:
• Things Fall Apart = clash of cultures, masculinity, fate
• Animal Farm = corruption of power, totalitarianism
✓ Final Answer: THEME is the soul of a work of fiction.

Question.content: One way to identify the characters in a work of fiction is to ask the question

Explanation.content:
👤 Who = Characters

🧐 The 5 Ws in Fiction:
• WHO? = characters (the people/beings in the story) ✅
• WHAT? = plot (what happens)
• WHERE? = setting (place)
• WHEN? = setting (time)
• WHY? = motivation/theme
✓ Final Answer: Ask WHO? to identify characters.

Question.content: What a character says is either called monologue or ______.

Explanation.content:
💬 Monologue vs Dialogue

🧐 Types of speech in fiction:
• Monologue = one character speaking alone (to self or audience) 🎭
• Dialogue = two or more characters speaking to each other 🗣️🗣️✅
❌ Not the others:
• Apologue = moral fable
• Alliteration = repetition of initial sounds
• Euphony = pleasant sound
✓ Final Answer: What a character says = monologue or DIALOGUE.

Question.content: The ______ is the main character in a work of narrative fiction.

Explanation.content:
🎭 Protagonist = Main Character

🧐 Character roles:
• Protagonist = main character, central figure ✅
• Antagonist = opposes the protagonist
• Villain = evil antagonist (not all antagonists are villains)
• Hero = protagonist with noble qualities (not all protagonists are heroes)
📖 Examples:
• Okonkwo = protagonist of Things Fall Apart
• Winston Smith = protagonist of 1984
✓ Final Answer: PROTAGONIST is the main character.

Question.content: "The sheets hanging on the clothesline danced in the wind" is an example of ______.

Explanation.content:
💃 Personification = Human Qualities to Non-Human

🔤 The sentence:
"The sheets danced in the wind."
🧐 What is personification?
• Giving human qualities/actions to non-human things ✅
• Sheets cannot literally dance — they are being personified!
🔄 Compare:
• Hyperbole = exaggeration
• Metonymy = substituting a related word
• Metaphor = direct comparison (no like/as)
✓ Final Answer: PERSONIFICATION.

Question.content: Which of the following is an example of hyperbole?
options: I have a million things to do today.
The vibrating washer caused the house to shake.

Answer.content: I have a million things to do today.

Explanation.content:
📈 Hyperbole = Exaggeration

🔍 Analyzing each option:
• ❌ "The wind grabbed my arm" = personification
• ❌ "The water is shiny" = literal observation
• ✅ "I have a million things to do" = HYPERBOLE (obvious exaggeration) ✅
• ❌ "The vibrating washer caused the house to shake" = possible literal statement
💡 Key:
Hyperbole is deliberate, obvious exaggeration not meant to be taken literally. No one literally has a million tasks!
✓ Final Answer: "I have a million things to do today" = HYPERBOLE.

Question.content: Which of the following best exemplifies an alliteration?
options: The silky snake slithered slowly sideways.
The snake was very slow and fat.
The frog jumped forward and landed upside-down.
The big bear had soft fur that was long and shaggy.

Answer.content: The silky snake slithered slowly sideways.

Explanation.content:
🔁 Alliteration = Repetition of Initial Sounds

🔍 Analyzing each option:
• ✅ The silky snake slithered slowly sideways = /s/ sound repeated ✅
• ❌ "The snake was very slow and fat" = no alliteration
• ❌ "The frog jumped forward..." = no alliteration
• ❌ "The big bear had soft fur..." = some repetition but not consistent
✓ Final Answer: "The silky snake slithered slowly sideways" = ALLITERATION.

Question.content: The sentence "He was a tornado, blasting his way through the opposing team" is an example of ______.

Explanation.content:
🌪️ Metaphor = Direct Comparison

🔤 The sentence:
"He was a tornado..."
🧐 Simile vs Metaphor:
• Simile = uses "like" or "as" (He was like a tornado)
• Metaphor = direct comparison, NO like/as (He was a tornado) ✅
❌ Not personification:
A tornado is not being given human qualities — a human is being compared to a tornado.
✓ Final Answer: METAPHOR.

Question.content: "The baby's skin was like a rose petal." What does this simile say to you?
options: The baby's skin is very soft and delicate.

Answer.content: The baby's skin is very soft and delicate.

Explanation.content:
🌹 Interpreting Similes

🔤 The simile:
"The baby's skin was like a rose petal."
🧐 What are rose petals like?
• Soft ✅
• Delicate ✅
• Smooth
• Tender
❌ Not the others:
• Rough ❌
• Dry ❌
• Sticky ❌
✓ Final Answer: The baby's skin is very soft and delicate.

Question.content: A good study group is made up of

Explanation.content:
👥 Ideal Study Group Size

🧐 Why not more than 6?
• Small groups (3-6) = everyone gets a chance to participate 🗣️
• Large groups (7+) = chaos, side conversations, some members silent or left out 🙊
• Too small (1-2) = limited perspectives and ideas 💭
📊 Benefits of small study groups:
• Everyone can contribute and ask questions ✅
• Easier to schedule meetings ✅
• More focused and productive sessions ✅
• Stronger accountability among members ✅
• Deeper discussions and understanding ✅
💡 Expert recommendation:
Most study skills experts and educational researchers recommend 4-6 members for optimal effectiveness. Groups larger than 6 become inefficient.
✓ Final Answer: A good study group = NOT MORE THAN 6 students.
